insights: TTR increased by 976% over 12 months—from 79.7 hours in January 2023 to 857.7 hours in December 2023—indicating a severe and accelerating degradation in resolution speed across the entire service operation.

The TTR trend is perfectly linear (R² = 0.9995) with a consistent increase of 72.1 hours per month (3.0 days/month), and every single month showed a >50-hour rise—confirming a monotonic, systemic deterioration rather than episodic spikes or recovery windows.

The TTR increase is universal: all 5 categories, 3 priority levels, and all assignment groups exhibit nearly identical slopes (71–102 hrs/month) and high R² (>0.97), ruling out segment-specific causes such as category complexity, priority handling delays, or team-level performance issues.

Time (month_num) is the dominant driver of TTR (r = 0.995), while categorical composition variables—including category, priority, assignment_group, and state—show negligible correlation with TTR (all r < 0.11), confirming that shifts in ticket mix are not contributing to the increase.

No evidence supports compositional explanation: the uniformity of TTR growth across segments—despite strong imbalances in baseline distribution (e.g., Network = 51.2%, Priority 2-High = 77.8%)—indicates the root cause lies outside ticket attributes and instead affects the end-to-end resolution process uniformly.

The systemic nature of the increase—spanning all operational dimensions without exception—points to an external or infrastructural factor (e.g., policy change, tooling degradation, staffing model shift, or upstream dependency failure) rather than localized inefficiencies or data artifacts.
summary: The dramatic 976% year-over-year increase in TTR is not attributable to changes in ticket composition, category complexity, priority distribution, or assignment group performance—it is a uniform, linear, and systemic degradation affecting every segment identically. With near-perfect correlation to time (r = 0.995) and negligible correlations to all categorical drivers (<0.11), the evidence conclusively points to a cross-cutting operational or environmental factor introduced gradually over 2023. This rules out tactical explanations like workload imbalance or skill gaps and strongly suggests a strategic-level change—such as a new SLA policy, degraded integration with upstream systems, reduced engineering bandwidth, or tooling limitations—that constrains resolution capacity across the board. The next step must focus on external context review: audit of process changes, staffing levels, system integrations, and policy updates between Q1 2023 and Q4 2023.
